item.ptxt: Using improper or contaminated brake fluid can cause brake system failure. Using shock absorber fluid or any other fluid that contains mineral oil for the brake fluid will cause the rubber parts in the brake hydraulic system to swell or distort. If water is mixed in the brake fluid, the boiling point will be lowered.
Use only the specified brake fluid from a sealed container.
Do not use a container that has been used for a mineral oil or is wet with water for refilling the brake system.
Keep all fluid containers capped to prevent contamination and entry of moisture.
Brake fluid is extremely damaging to painted surfaces.
If brake fluid contacts a painted surface, immediately wipe it off and flush the surface thoroughly with water.
Be sure to use brake fluid indicated on brake master cylinder reservoir cap (of the vehicle) or in owner’s manual.
Check that brake fluid level is between “MAX” and “MIN” marks on reservoir of brake master cylinder.

attention3.ptxt: If brake warning light comes on during driving or brake fluid level on brake master cylinder reservoir is “MIN” mark or lower, check the following points:

item.ptxt: Thickness of front brake pads and rear brake pads
Fluid leakage from piston seal of front brake caliper
Fluid leakage from piston seal of rear brake caliper
Cracks or damage to brake hoses and brake pipes
Damage to clutch hose and clutch pipe
Fluid leakage from brake master cylinder and reservoir

attention3.ptxt: In case any abnormality is found, repair or replace affected parts.
After the check and repair are completed, replenish brake fluid so that fluid level is fall between “MAX” and “MIN” marks on brake master cylinder reservoir.
